clean_story:
No inherent meaning. Freedom as burden.

The word existentialism comes from existence—the raw fact of being alive, without a prewritten script. It’s like waking up in a video game with no instructions, no quest log, just an open world and the weight of choosing your next move.

Existentialists argue you’re not born with a purpose. Instead, you build one through actions—like sculpting clay that hardens only after you’ve shaped it. Jean-Paul Sartre called this "radical freedom": every choice, even small ones (ignoring a stranger, picking a career), carves your identity. No excuses. No divine plan. Just consequences.

Take Albert Camus’ Myth of Sisyphus: a man doomed to roll a boulder uphill forever. The absurdity isn’t the task—it’s his refusal to quit. Existentialism says: embrace the struggle. Create meaning in the climb.

Three related subjects are absurdism, humanist ethics, and how art confronts nothingness.
